definition: To presage is to portend — for a sign or event to point ahead to what will follow; as a noun, a presage is that foreboding sign itself. Unlike the inward, felt *premonition*, a presage is read outward, in omens and portents; unlike the neutral *prognostic*, it leans toward warning and dread.
history: The word comes from Latin *praesagium*, 'a foreboding', from *prae-* ('before') and *sagire*, 'to perceive keenly' — the same keen-scenting root behind *sagacious*. In the literature of magic it names the omen that reaches forward: an eclipse that presaged the Sultan's death in John Dee's world (Parry, *The Arch Conjuror of England*), or the trivial occurrences God might send 'to presage success or failure' (Thomas, *Religion and the Decline of Magic*). To demonologists, the very flourishing of witches was 'a presage of the coming desolation' of the faith (Clark, *Thinking with Demons*).

def_sources.label: Glyn Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England: John Dee
def_sources.abstract: A biography arguing that John Dee was not just an eccentric magician on the sidelines of history, but a real political operator whose magic and angel-conversations were tangled up with Elizabethan court intrigue and Europe's religious conflicts.

def_sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
def_sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

def_sources.label: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons
def_sources.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

etymology.origin: Latin praesagium 'a foreboding' (prae- + sagire 'to perceive keenly')
etymology.note: The keen nose for what's coming; the sagire root also yields 'sagacious'.
etymology.hook: A sharp nose for the future.

flavor_snippets.html: The passing of the sai <mark>presages</mark> the end of the old clan and the ascent of the new.
